name: Franklin Falls - Main Falls Lines
height: 70 ft main, 135 ft total with tiers
watch_out: Avalanche terrain: Steep south-facing slope above belay area; climbers in direct line of potential slides
Chandelier ice: Brittle, unstable ice in top half of routes; prone to collapse in warm weather
Spray ice: River spray ice is low-density and deceptive; boot penetration leads to water immersion
Loose rock and ice: Area experiences heavy hiker traffic; rockfall and ice chunks from other parties
Communication hazard: Waterfall noise + freeway roar make belay communication impossible; requires pre-climb plan
Mixed access: Left side routes have half-dozen lines but treacherous approach on low-density spray ice
Descent: Right-side mixed routes add technical variation; route-finding critical
ice_quality: Sturdy lower half, brittle upper chandelier ice; forms during sustained freeze cycles; rare for main falls to be fully in condition
seasonal_detail: Left and right subsidiary lines freeze almost every year during cold spells; main central falls rarely forms completely
weather_patterns: South-facing exposure makes afternoon sun hazard; needs clear winter pattern with stable cold
sources: Franklin Falls Ice Climbing - The Mountaineers trip report

name: Alpental Falls
watch_out: Avalanche danger from above: Substantial terrain above fall lines can produce slides; rightmost lines provide best protection
Thin, funky ice: Patches of thin, water-covered ice interspersed with solid sections; risk of punching through to running water or bare rock
Snow cover: Thin snow layer obscures ice quality; climb assessments difficult from below
Crux finish: Final pitch grades WI3 curtain after easy WI2 climbing; abrupt difficulty change
Rope management: 60m rope doesn't reach ground; multi-pitch descent required; crevasse rescue considerations
Seasonal ephemeral: Highly moisture-sensitive; forms only during sustained freeze cycles following coastal maritime patterns
ice_quality: WI2 bulk with WI3 crux final curtain; thin and variable quality; requires 5+ days sustained below-freezing
approach: 0.5 mile hike from Snow Lake trailhead parking; relatively accessible for major route
sources: Climber Kyle - Alpental Falls + Phantom Tour (Feb 2024)

name: Alpental Practice Wall
watch_out: Low-angle terrain: Minimal protection; focus is practice and skill building rather than serious climbing
Top-rope setup potential: Can be arranged; assumes some bolted/equipped anchors available
Seasonal: Beginner-friendly formation requirements; more reliable than major falls
ice_quality: WI1-2, mostly under WI2; reliable practice-level climbing
description: Designed practice area with non-committing pitches suitable for top-rope setup; steepest grade WI2

name: Kiddie Cliff
pitches: Single pitch per line (left WI4, right WI3)
watch_out: Substantial avalanche hazard above: Steep terrain overhead; avalanche forecast must be checked before climbing
Inadequate rappel anchor: 70m rope from tree anchor will NOT reach ground; extended rappels or complex descent required
Snow mushroom summit: Lead on left line complicated by unstable top-out; loose snow/ice at anchor
Top-rope feasible: Can be safely top-roped from tree anchors; avoids summit exposure and rappel issues
Shaded, low-volume flow: Forms more frequently than high-volume falls but still seasonal; sustained cold required
ice_quality: Lower-volume, shaded aspect; WI3-4 with shale/mixed climbing character; forms more reliably than major falls
approach: 20 min walk from Source Lake exit; moderate approach
description: Two distinct lines: left (WI4) = crux line with difficult top-out; right (WI3) = more moderate

name: Dirty Harry's Ice
watch_out: South-facing exposure: Extreme seasonal sensitivity; only forms during rare sustained deep-freeze events
Ephemeral formation: Requires extreme cold (highs in mid-20s, lows in single digits) for 5+ days; once-per-decade occurrence
Tight gully terrain: Steep approach with bushwhacking; route-finding challenges in snow
Variable ice: Mix of low-angle (30-45 deg) and vertical sections; ice quality depends on formation sequence
Limited access window: Formed only during 'Great Freeze' type events; not a reliable destination
ice_quality: Rambly, variable angle; most is low-angle (30-45 deg) with vertical sections; depends on extreme sustained cold
approach: 1 hr 10 min hike from turnoff, moderate bushwhacking required, near Exit 38 and Dirty Harry's Balcony
description: One-pitch ephemeral route; relies on rare extreme freeze cycles; not recommended as primary destination

name: Hot Tubbs (Bryant Peak)
pitch_details: P1: 20m WI4 - Vertical water ice crux right off start
P2: 45m WI3 - Ice flows with bulge, moderate snow to tree belay
P3: 75m, 50-deg snow - Snowfield with trend toward cliff notch
P4: 40m, 60-70 deg snow - Steep north-face traverse on cliffs beneath
watch_out: Crux pitch: Immediate WI4 climbing; steep rock-hard water ice may require aid or dry-tooling
Sustained snow climbing: Pitches 3-4 involve 50-70 degree slopes; cornices and rime ice common
North face exposure: Steep snow traverse with cliffs beneath; limited fall-zone recovery
Avalanche exposure: Approach and early pitches cross terrain with overhead slide risk
Weather sensitivity: Route prone to icing and wind slab formation on north face
Descent logistics: Need ropes for rappel descent; establish anchors carefully
ice_quality: WI4 pillar then WI3 ice flows + snow; quality water ice in early season; rime ice later season
approach: Access via Denny Creek Trail or over Bryant Col; 2-3 hr approach
sources: Climber Kyle - New Snoqualmie Pass Ice Climbs (Jan 2019)

name: Melakwa Flows (Bryant Peak West Face)
watch_out: Large snowfield above: Significant avalanche terrain; must assess stability and avoid during high-hazard periods
Numerous gullies: Dozens of minor flows can create complex terrain and route confusion in whiteout
Steep ice: WI4 climbing on sustained vertical sections; good gear placement critical
Variable ice quality: Multiple flows mean different formation timing and quality; reconnaissance needed
Serac potential: Freezing/thawing cycles in spring can destabilize flow tops
Descent complexity: Multiple rappels required; anchor quality variable on unknown routes
ice_quality: WI4 sustained vertical ice; densest concentration of water ice in region; variable quality between flows
description: Amphitheater of flows with at least a dozen distinct routes, 150-300 ft tall; one of best concentrations of accessible ice in Washington
approach: Access via Denny Creek Trail or over Bryant Col from Alpental
sources: Climber Kyle - New Snoqualmie Pass Ice Climbs

name: Rap Wall - Hotline Ramp
watch_out: Variable ice quality: Despite 'ramp' name, first 15 feet dead vertical; quality varies by line choice
Tricky traversing moves: Crux involves tactical ice tool placements on thin sections
Pillar variation: Main pillar section can form WI5-WI6; route choice critical
Formation timing: Forms 'rather slowly but consistently'; survives multiple warm-rain events
Exposure: Limited fall zone but exposed terrain; proper protection essential
ice_quality: WI3 ramp transitioning to vertical; thin to solid ice depending on formation; rarely reaches pillar crux
approach: ~2 miles, ~1,200 ft elevation gain from Source Lake; ~1 hr approach
location_detail: 500 ft above Source Lake, eastern aspect; consistent formation area

name: Rap Wall - Hotline Pillar
watch_out: Extreme difficulty: One of hardest ice pitches in Washington; technical climbing for elite climbers
Overhanging start: Slightly overhanging entry with thin feet; pull-ups and lockoffs required
Equipment demands: Specialized tools, approach shoes, and advanced protection knowledge necessary
Formation dependent: Thin ice conditions; minimal protection placements; high consequences of falls
Weather sensitive: Rime ice and wind-loading common; not recommended in poor conditions
ice_quality: WI5-6+ thin ice; overhanging character; elite-level climbing
description: One of hardest ice pitches in Washington state; elite-only destination
sources: Climber Kyle - Hotline Ramp and Rap Wall assessment (Jan 2023)

name: Rap Wall - The 36th Chamber
watch_out: Mixed climbing complexity: Combines ice and rock; gear placement on mixed terrain difficult
Chimney formation: Tight climbing with potential for pack/body jamming
R-rated protection: Limited/serious gear opportunities; high consequence climbing
Expert-only route: Requires advanced mixed climbing skills and gear placement ability
First-ascent implications: Route quality and hazard information limited by FA status
ice_quality: WI5+ technical ice in mixed/chimney setting
description: Serious mixed climbing route; R-rated protection; elite climbers only

name: Rap Wall - Bring Da Ruckus
watch_out: Expert-only difficulty: WI6+ grade indicates elite ice climbing and aid mixed climbing
M8+ climbing: High-grade rock and dry-tool climbing; extreme technical skill required
First ascent: Route information limited; exact hazards not fully documented
Serious protection: Likely PG-13 rating reflects sketchy anchor/protection options
Not recommended for most parties: Only experienced alpine climbers in top condition
description: Modern hard mixed route; first ascent by elite Cascades climbers; expert-only

name: Chair Peak - Northeast Buttress
watch_out: Mixed terrain: Rock scrambling with ice/snow climbing; protection placement variable
Avalanche hazard: Alpine terrain with overhead slopes; must assess snowpack stability
Winter-specific hazard: Rime ice common on exposed faces; visibility and wind major factors
Descent: Alpine route descent requires route-finding skills in whiteout
Exposure: Serious alpine terrain; bivvy/rescue implications
ice_quality: AI2-3 alpine ice mixed with rock scrambling
description: Classic moderate alpine climbing; good winter mountaineering objective from Snoqualmie Pass
sources: Mountain Project - Chair Peak NE Buttress
